title: Linear vs Non-linear Boundaries
concept: Linear boundaries are straight lines that separate classes, while non-linear boundaries are curves or shapes needed for complex datasets.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What happens to a classification model's predictions if you lower the decision threshold from 0.5 to 0.2?

options.text: The model will classify more cases as 'yes' (1), flagging even low-probability events
The model will classify fewer cases as 'yes' (1), requiring higher certainty
The model's predictions will not change at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Lowering the threshold makes the model more sensitive, flagging more cases as 'yes' even if the probability is low. Now, let's look at the shape of these boundaries.

cards.title: Watermelon sorting
cards.body: Imagine sorting seeds from fruit. If the seeds are all in the center and the sweet fruit is on the outside, you can't separate them with a single straight knife cut. You need a circular cut.

cards.title: Boundary types, defined
cards.body: A linear boundary is a straight line (or flat plane) that separates classes. A non-linear boundary is curved, wavy, or shaped like a circle or wiggle to divide complex data.

cards.title: Backyard fence
cards.body: Think of a backyard fence. A linear boundary is a straight wooden fence dividing two flat yards. A non-linear boundary is a curved stone wall wrapping around a garden pond to keep dogs out of the water.

cards.title: Bending the line
cards.body: Logistic regression naturally creates a straight decision boundary (linear). To separate a red ring of points surrounding a blue core, we must feed the model squared features ($x^2, y^2$), bending the boundary into a circle.

cards.title: Myth: Curved is always better
cards.body: False. Curved boundaries can easily overfit, capturing noise instead of the real pattern. Straight lines are often more robust.

cards.title: Comparing boundary shapes
cards.body: Linear and non-linear boundaries solve different types of classification challenges:

left.text:
Straight line or flat plane
Simple, robust, default in logistic regression

right.text:
Curved, circular, or complex
Handles nested or complex data patterns

exercises.prompt: What kind of decision boundary does standard logistic regression create?

options.text: A linear (straight) boundary
A perfectly circular boundary
A wavy, non-linear boundary
It doesn't create any boundary

exercises.explain: Standard logistic regression creates a linear decision boundary (a straight line in 2D, or a flat plane in higher dimensions).

exercises.prompt: True or false: To create a curved decision boundary, we can transform our features (e.g., squaring them) before feeding them to the model.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! By adding feature interactions or squared terms, we can model non-linear boundaries using linear algorithms.

exercises.sentence: If blue dots form a tight circle inside a ring of red dots, a ___ boundary is required to separate them successfully.
exercises.explain: A straight line (linear) would cut through both classes. Only a curved or circular (non-linear) boundary can isolate the center.

exercises.prompt: Which dataset absolutely requires a non-linear decision boundary to separate the two classes?

options.text: A dataset where class A forms a checkerboard pattern interleaved with class B
A dataset where class A is in the top-left and class B is in the bottom-right
A dataset where class A and B are perfectly separated by a horizontal gap

exercises.explain: A checkerboard pattern has multiple alternating regions, which cannot be separated by a single straight line; it requires non-linear boundaries.

exercises.prompt: What is a major risk of using highly complex, wiggly non-linear boundaries?

options.text: Overfitting to noise in the training data
Underestimating the number of features
Forcing the model to only output 0.5 probability
The model becoming too simple to use

exercises.explain: Complex boundaries can easily 'wiggle' around every single noise point, leading to overfitting and poor performance on new data.

exercises.prompt: You are classifying donut shapes: the inner hole is 'doughnut hole' and the outer ring is 'doughnut cake'. A linear classifier fails. What is the most efficient way to solve this?

options.text: Transform the inputs to include the distance from the center ($x^2 + y^2$) to create a circular boundary
Force a straight line through the center and accept a 50% error rate
Add thousands of random features until the line happens to fit
Switch to a regression model and predict the size of the donut instead

exercises.explain: Brilliant! Calculating the distance from the center (like $x^2 + y^2$) lets a simple model draw a circular decision boundary, perfectly separating the hole from the cake. You've conquered non-linear boundaries!
